text: In 2020 I had just launched my web design business and had zero clients. I had no interest in hunting people down and begging them to pay me, so I started posting random tips on TikTok, crossing my fingers someone would reach out. And it actually worked - leads started showing up in my inbox.

I hit this cycle where when I was posting, things were great - steady stream of clients. But I'd burn out fast, stop posting, and everything would dry up. My follower count kept growing but my income stayed stuck at $10K a month. More audience, same money.

I spent months building out a web design template - the product, all the tutorials, everything ready to go. Then I posted about it once and never talked about it again. That was one of about five things I tried over two years that went absolutely nowhere.
source_episode: the web design template that died

text: In March 2023 I quit. I stopped taking new clients, scrapped my entire business, and started from scratch. This time I decided I was done bending over backwards for the algorithm. I was just going to serve my audience and figure out what they actually needed.
For six months after I quit, I made zero money. By September 2023 I was completely broke. I was on the phone to my mum, in tears, telling her I was ready to give up.

The week after that phone call with my mum, I had my first successful launch. $1,500 in the first 24 hours. After six months of nothing, fifteen hundred dollars felt like proof that something was actually working.
By February 2024 I hit $41,202 in a single month. The month after that, $52,503. Less than a year earlier I'd been on the phone crying to my mum with zero income.

I had five YouTube videos that were bringing in $20K a month on autopilot. Videos I posted once. No team, no complex funnels, no churning out endless content. Just the right offer and a system that kept selling while I was offline.

For two years I kept testing things - day rates, a course, digital products, the template. Every time I thought I'd found the answer and every time it fizzled. I was copying what other people were doing and none of it fit how my brain actually works.

I quit my first YouTube channel at 41,000 subscribers. Walked away from it. People thought I was insane but it wasn't aligned anymore - I was building someone else's version of success and I could feel it.

The whole time I kept telling myself something was wrong with me. Everyone else could be consistent and I couldn't. If I were just better, I'd be further along. Turns out my brain wasn't broken - I was just trying to run someone else's playbook.

First year on YouTube I made $320,080. To date, $349,316 in digital product sales. And the biggest lesson wasn't about money - it was that I didn't need a bigger audience. I already had enough people. The work was converting them, not chasing more reach.
